subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s degree from a four-year university
Major likely in a field related to technology, business, or nonprofit management
No advanced degrees yet (e.g., Master's or PhD)
Participated in several project-based courses during undergraduate studies
Completed coursework in computer science and project management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Co-creator and fundraiser for alphaXiv, an academic collaboration tool
Role encompasses product feature brainstorming, coding, and stakeholder communication
Actively involved in both product development and external relationship management
Engages in direct discussions with users and advisors to inform next steps

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the technology sector, specifically in educational technology (EdTech)
alphaXiv targets academic and research collaboration markets
Operates at the intersection of software development and nonprofit/fundraising

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works in a small startup environment with a tight-knit team
Team likely consists of fewer than 10 full-time members
Collaborative and informal workplace culture
Frequent in-person discussions and idea exchanges

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: alphaXiv operates as a SaaS (Software as a Service) platform for academic collaboration
Targets universities, research labs, and academic professionals
Business model may involve partnerships or grants, given fundraising responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has less than 1 year of professional experience in current role
Began working as co-creator/fundraiser shortly after graduation
First full-time role post-undergraduate studies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Entry-level professional, early in career trajectory
Assumes significant responsibility due to startup context
Not yet in a formal managerial or senior leadership role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Brainstorms and outlines new product features for alphaXiv
Writes and tests code for platform development
Communicates regularly with users and advisors to gather feedback
Plans next development steps based on team discussions
Participates in meetings to align on strategy and tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Time is distributed fairly evenly across several dozen tasks
No single task dominates the workweek
Roughly equal allocation among brainstorming, coding, and communications
Flexibility to shift focus as new priorities emerge

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: High degree of autonomy due to startup structure and co-creator status
Directly influences feature prioritization and technical implementation
Collaborates closely with teammates but contributes significantly to decision-making
Consults with advisors and users for input, but has discretion in day-to-day choices

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Coding straightforward features or small proof-of-concept implementations feels natural
Engaging in collaborative brainstorming sessions is enjoyable and intuitive
Communicating with peers in informal discussions comes easily
Synthesizing user feedback into actionable next steps is manageable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Balancing multiple simultaneous responsibilities can be difficult
Coordinating between technical development and user/stakeholder communication poses challenges
Planning long-term product roadmaps with limited experience is complex
Addressing integration issues between new features and existing codebase may be demanding

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Data entry related to user feedback or fundraising contacts is repetitive
Testing and debugging minor code changes could be automated
Tracking and categorizing support requests is data-heavy and potentially automatable
Generating routine status update emails or reports is suitable for automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys brainstorming new product features and discussing ideas with the team
Finds coding new functionalities engaging, especially when using AI tools for speed
Tedious tasks include manual data entry and administrative paperwork
Repetitive reporting or organizing meeting notes is less motivating

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Switching frequently between diverse tasks can disrupt focus
Manual tracking of user feedback and action items is prone to oversight
Occasional technical issues with tool integration slow down workflows
Limited experience sometimes makes prioritization less efficient

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher enjoyment correlates with tasks where skill is higher (e.g., coding, brainstorming)
Productivity peaks when working on engaging, skill-aligned projects
Tedious or less-developed skills (e.g., admin work) are less enjoyable and slower
Learning new tasks can be enjoyable if there's clear progress and support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Proactive and enthusiastic about adopting new tools, especially AI-based ones
Views new technology as an opportunity for efficiency and innovation
Open to experimenting with beta features and early releases
Sees learning new tools as integral to personal and team growth

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new software, especially those relevant to coding and collaboration
Regularly explores new tools to enhance workflow
Willing to shift methodologies if they prove more effective
Comfortable with ongoing changes in the tech stack

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate time to learning tools that offer clear benefits
Prefers hands-on, self-guided exploration over formal, lengthy training sessions
Balances training time with immediate project needs
Would prioritize training if endorsed by peers or advisors

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential to speed up development or improve product quality
Learning is most appealing when directly applicable to current tasks
Heavy workload can limit time available for in-depth learning
Tools that lack immediate relevance are less likely to be adopted

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peer recommendations strongly influence tool adoption
Team culture encourages experimentation with new tech
Advisor input can accelerate adoption of proven tools
Management’s hands-off approach allows individual initiative

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses GitHub for code collaboration and version control
Employs Slack or Discord for team communication
Works in Visual Studio Code or a similar IDE for programming tasks
Relies on Google Workspace (Docs, Sheets) for documentation and planning
Regularly interacts with LLM-based coding assistants (e.g., GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT)

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Uses Trello or Jira for task and project management
Communicates with users and advisors via email and scheduled video calls (e.g., Zoom)
Maintains documentation and meeting notes in Google Docs
Tracks fundraising contacts in a basic CRM or spreadsheet

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient in web development frameworks (e.g., React, Node.js)
Comfortable with using REST APIs for integrations
Experienced with cloud-based collaboration tools
Familiar with basic SQL or NoSQL databases

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasional challenges syncing data between task management and documentation platforms
Integrating LLM-based code suggestions into existing workflows sometimes requires manual adjustment
Switching between multiple communication channels can lead to missed messages
Versioning conflicts may arise when collaborating on shared codebases

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Comfortable with core concepts like machine learning, natural language processing, and generative AI
Understands the basics of LLMs and prompt engineering
Familiar with AI terms such as model training, inference, and fine-tuning
Keeps up-to-date with new AI trends relevant to software development

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT for coding assistance and brainstorming
Employs GitHub Copilot as an integrated code suggestion tool
May use Claude or similar LLMs for drafting communications
Experiments with AI-powered productivity extensions in VS Code

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies AI tools daily for coding new features or quick prototypes
Uses LLMs to generate boilerplate code and debug errors
Employs AI for drafting initial user emails or reports
Consults AI for brainstorming feature ideas or summarizing meeting notes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI has successfully accelerated proof-of-concept development for new features
Occasionally, AI-generated code requires manual correction to fit project standards
LLMs sometimes miss context in multi-step tasks, leading to incomplete outputs
Learned the importance of reviewing and validating all AI-generated outputs before deployment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal organizational AI training; learning is primarily self-driven
Peers share tips and best practices informally
Occasional group discussions on optimizing AI tool usage
Relies on online tutorials and documentation to improve AI tool proficiency

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers interactive, step-by-step collaboration with AI tools for complex tasks
Uses AI independently for straightforward or well-defined coding tasks
Switches between modes depending on the problem scope
Values the ability to iterate quickly with AI feedback

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as a coding assistant or brainstorming partner
Occasionally treats AI as an advisor for generating new ideas
Sees AI as a co-worker for repetitive or automatable tasks
Maintains human oversight over all AI-generated outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Very open to experimenting with new AI-driven workflows in coding and prototyping
More cautious about automating planning or workflow decisions with AI
Willing to pilot AI-based processes if they show clear value
Looks for peer validation before widespread adoption of new workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational AI interfaces for brainstorming and problem-solving
Uses command-based interfaces for precise coding or technical queries
Switches interface style based on task complexity
Finds conversational UIs more intuitive for exploring new ideas

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Some tasks, like stakeholder communication, require deep understanding of academic culture
Product planning often depends on nuanced knowledge of user needs
Technical coding tasks may benefit from general software expertise rather than domain-specific knowledge
Relies on personal judgment when addressing ambiguous or sensitive issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for generating initial code drafts but always reviews before deployment
Skeptical of AI recommendations for strategic planning or user communications
Treats AI outputs as suggestions rather than final decisions
Increases scrutiny for critical or high-impact tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers human oversight for planning, user engagement, and critical code merges
Favors AI automation for repetitive, low-stakes, or time-consuming coding tasks
Uses AI to augment, not replace, human creativity in brainstorming
Seeks a workflow where AI accelerates output but humans ensure quality

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for non-critical, routine coding or admin tasks
Finds full automation of user communications or fundraising decisions potentially risky
Considers high automation threatening when it reduces transparency or control
Requires the option for manual review and intervention in all automated processes

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances value of critical thinking and problem-solving skills
Routine coding skills may be less central as AI handles boilerplate tasks
Communication and interpersonal skills remain highly important
Sees AI as a tool to amplify, not replace, existing capabilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Prompt engineering and crafting effective AI queries are now key skills
Ability to evaluate and validate AI outputs has become essential
Staying updated on AI trends and tool capabilities is a new responsibility
Integrating AI into team workflows requires new coordination skills

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Minimal concern about AI replacing core creative or relational aspects of the role
Recognizes that some repetitive coding or admin tasks may become obsolete
Watches industry trends but feels secure due to multi-faceted job scope
Believes human skills in relationship management and planning are not easily automated

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews and edits AI-generated code before merging
Manual approval required for any AI-generated user communication
Prefers systems that allow for easy rollback of AI-driven changes
No critical tasks are fully automated without human intervention

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team informally encourages exploration of AI tools
No formal policy restricting or mandating AI use
Peers regularly share new AI tools and findings
Company is open but cautious about automating high-impact processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as a major driver of efficiency and innovation in tech and academia
Believes AI can democratize access to knowledge and collaboration
Recognizes potential for AI to disrupt traditional job structures
Cautiously optimistic about AI’s role in supporting, not replacing, human work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports responsible AI deployment with human oversight
Concerned about bias and lack of transparency in some AI systems
Believes ethical guidelines are needed for AI use in sensitive contexts
Advocates for clear boundaries on what AI should and shouldn’t automate

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from AI tools that can assist with workflow and long-term planning
Desires more context-aware AI assistants for stakeholder communications
Wants better integration between AI tools and existing project management platforms
Hopes for tools that automate tedious admin tasks without losing customization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine coding and admin work to be largely automated
Foresees increased focus on creativity, user engagement, and strategic planning
Believes new roles will emerge around AI tool management and oversight
Anticipates a shift toward more high-level problem-solving responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Encourage ongoing experimentation with new AI tools and workflows
Develop guidelines for ethical and responsible AI use
Invest in training and resources to help team members upskill in AI
Foster a culture of knowledge sharing around AI best practices
